commonIssues: Botrytis (grey mould) on fruit and crown, worsened by overhead watering or soggy soil; water at the base in the morning and avoid wetting the crown
Shallow roots dry out fast, especially in containers, so containers need frequent watering
Frost during flowering blackens and kills open blooms even though dormant plants are hardy; cover with row cover or cloche when frost threatens flowers
Birds, slugs, snails, aphids, spotted wing drosophila and vine weevil; anthracnose, verticillium wilt and viruses
Below roughly -6 C (20 F) plants go dormant and benefit from a ~4 inch straw mulch for winter protection

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center, Growing Strawberries (HGIC 1404): full sun; 1 to 1.5 inches of water per week wetting soil 6 to 8 inches deep; soil pH 6.0 to 6.5; annual hill spacing 12 inches; nitrogen side-dress about one month after planting and post-renovation feeding mid-August to mid-September; harvest March to May by region; problems anthracnose, Botrytis (gray mold), nematodes, birds.

Royal Horticultural Society, How to grow strawberries: full sun for sweetest fruit (tolerates some shade); avoid wetting the crown and water in the morning to prevent grey mould; containers dry out quickly and need regular watering; feed container plants fortnightly with a general-purpose then high-potassium liquid feed once flower buds form; plant spring, late summer or early autumn (Aug/Sep best); summer-fruiting crop early June to early August; hardy but cover flowers/early fruit if frost forecast; problems include grey mould, aphids, slugs/snails, spotted wing drosophila, vine weevil, verticillium wilt and viruses.

The Old Farmer's Almanac, How to Grow Strawberries: requires 6 to 10 hours of direct sunlight a day; day-neutral types fruit while temperatures stay between 35 and 85 F (1 to 30 C); super-hardy but frost turns flowers to blackened mush; plants go dormant when temperatures regularly drop into the low twenties F, cut foliage to ~1 inch when air reaches 20 F (-6 C) and mulch ~4 inches deep for winter protection.

EarthBox, Growing Strawberries in Containers: a single strawberry plant needs a pot at least 8 to 10 inches deep and roughly 12 to 18 inches wide; a 6 to 8 inch pot holds 1 plant, a 10 to 12 inch pot 2 to 3 plants, and a 5-gallon (~19 L) grow bag about 3 plants. Used to bound container minimum (~4 L single small pot), ideal min ~8 L, and ideal max ~19 L.

Confidence note: container liters are converted from inch/gallon pot guidance (no extension source states liters directly); recommendedMinLiters=4 retains the proven seed value and reflects a single-plant ~1-gallon small pot, with idealMin/Max (8-19 L) bracketing multi-plant pots. coldHardyMinC=-6 C is set to the Almanac's 20 F foliage/dormancy threshold as a conservative active-growth floor rather than the deeper temperature dormant crowns can survive under mulch. verticalSuitability=0.7 reflects strawberries' well-documented suitability for towers/hanging baskets but is an interpretive score, not a sourced number. hotWeatherMultiplier=1.5 is an engine estimate for shallow-rooted, low-drying-tolerance plants, not a published figure.
